(2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto,'' p. 141.</ref> Prior to Kanetomo, the understanding and practice of Shinto was intermingled with [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]].<ref name="titsingh368"/> Sanetomo invested a lifetime in a process of disentangling what were thereafter construed as the two distinct entities.<ref name="ito"/>
===Inverted ''honji suijaku''=== {{Main|Honji suijaku}} The term ''[[honji suijaku]]'' expresses a Japanese [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] theory according to which a perceived Shinto ''[[kami]]'' is the manifestation of a Buddhist god.<ref>Satō Masato. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1356 "''Honji Suijaku Setsu'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007; ''Basic Terms of Shinto'', [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_h.html#honji-suijaku_setsu "Honji Suijaku Setsu,"] 1997.</ref> This theory proposed and presumed that the resulting dual entity would necessarily have a fundamental Buddhist core, and that any Shinto aspect was secondary.<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. [http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm "''Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami'';"] Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
In the late [[Kamakura period]], a counter-theory arose which also began with the notion of such dual entities; however, the counter-theorists construed that the ''kami'' side was primary and the Buddhist one was secondary. This came to be known as the ''Inverted honji suijaku ''.<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
Kanetomo was influenced by these ideas and brought them further. He proposed to set aside the conceptual theories of such entities.<ref>Shirayama, Yoshitarō. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1355 "''Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu'' ('Anti-Honjisuijaku thought')."] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007.</ref>
Up through the end of the [[Edo]] period, Kanetomo's followers and the [[Yoshida Shrine]] were granted the right to award ranks to all shrines and priests except for those associated with the Imperial family.<ref>Breen, John et al. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 176.|page=176}}</ref>

{{family name hatnote|Yoshida|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Nijuunisha-chuushiki.JPG|250 px|thumb|A copy of the Nijūnisha Chūshiki by Yoshida Kanetomo]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshida Kanetomo'''|吉田 兼倶||1435–1511}} was a Japanese [[Shinto]] priest of the [[Sengoku period]]. He was a seminal figure in the evolution of a coherent descriptive and interpretive [[Model (abstract)|schema]] of Shinto ritual and [[mythology]].<ref name="ito">Itō Satoshi. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo,"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' April 15, 2006.</ref>
==Career== Kanetomo progressed gradually through the ranks of the Imperial offices in the [[Jingi-kan]] (Department of Shinto Affairs), which was one of the Imperial bureaucracies which were set up under the [[ritsuryō]] system in the 8th century. Kanetomo's eventually became an {{nihongo|Imperial Chamberlain|侍従|jijū}}. Other positions he held at different times were {{nihongo|Senior Assistant Director of Divinities|神祇大輔|jingi taifu}} and {{nihongo|Vice Director of the Board of Censors|弾正台弼|danjōdai hitsu}}.<ref name="ito"/>
*'''1511''' (''[[Eishō (Muromachi period)|Eishō]] 8, 2nd month''): When Kanetomo died at the age of 77, his passing was considered a significant event in the chronicles of the Imperial [[history of Japan]].<ref name="titsingh368">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834) {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 368.|page=368}}; aka Ourabe no Kane tomo</ref>
==Yoshida Shinto== The early period Shinto school founded by Kanetomo was called ''Genpon-Sōgen Shinto'' ("Shinto of the Original Founder"),<ref name="ito"/> also known as Yuiitsu Shintō ("Only one Shintō").<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto,'' p. 141.</ref> Prior to Kanetomo, the understanding and practice of Shinto was intermingled with [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]].<ref name="titsingh368"/> Sanetomo invested a lifetime in a process of disentangling what were thereafter construed as the two distinct entities.<ref name="ito"/>
===Inverted ''honji suijaku''=== {{Main|Honji suijaku}} The term ''[[honji suijaku]]'' expresses a Japanese [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] theory according to which a perceived Shinto ''[[kami]]'' is the manifestation of a Buddhist god.<ref>Satō Masato. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1356 "''Honji Suijaku Setsu'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007; ''Basic Terms of Shinto'', [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_h.html#honji-suijaku_setsu "Honji Suijaku Setsu,"] 1997.</ref> This theory proposed and presumed that the resulting dual entity would necessarily have a fundamental Buddhist core, and that any Shinto aspect was secondary.<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. [http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm "''Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami'';"] Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
In the late [[Kamakura period]], a counter-theory arose which also began with the notion of such dual entities; however, the counter-theorists construed that the ''kami'' side was primary and the Buddhist one was secondary. This came to be known as the ''Inverted honji suijaku ''.<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
Kanetomo was influenced by these ideas and brought them further. He proposed to set aside the conceptual theories of such entities.<ref>Shirayama, Yoshitarō. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1355 "''Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu'' ('Anti-Honjisuijaku thought')."] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007.</ref>
Up through the end of the [[Edo]] period, Kanetomo's followers and the [[Yoshida Shrine]] were granted the right to award ranks to all shrines and priests except for those associated with the Imperial family.<ref>Breen, John et al. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 176.|page=176}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Shinto sects and schools]] * [[Kunitokotachi]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Bernhard Scheid|Scheid, Bernhard]]. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto.'' Vienna: ''Verlag der Österreichischen Akademie der Wissenschaften''. {{ISBN|9783700129899}}; {{OCLC|186460367}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''] (''[[Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''). Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}}
==External links== * [[Kokugakuin University]], ''Encyclopedia of Shinto,'' [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo"]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:]]" to "[[Category:Pages whose categories were destroyed by the bot]]"
revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Yoshida|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Nijuunisha-chuushiki.JPG|250 px|thumb|A copy of the Nijūnisha Chūshiki by Yoshida Kanetomo]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshida Kanetomo'''|吉田 兼倶||1435–1511}} was a Japanese [[Shinto]] priest of the [[Sengoku period]]. He was a seminal figure in the evolution of a coherent descriptive and interpretive [[Model (abstract)|schema]] of Shinto ritual and [[mythology]].<ref name="ito">Itō Satoshi. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo,"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' April 15, 2006.</ref>
==Career== Kanetomo progressed gradually through the Ranks of the Imperial offices in the [[Jingi-kan]] (Department of Shinto Affairs), which was one of the Imperial bureaucracies which were set up under the [[ritsuryō]] system in the 8th century. Kanetomo's eventually became an {{nihongo|Imperial Chamberlain|侍従|jijū}}. Other positions he held at different times were {{nihongo|Senior Assistant Director of Divinities|神祇大輔|jingi taifu}} and {{nihongo|Vice Director of the Board of Censors|弾正台弼|danjōdai hitsu}}.<ref name="ito"/>
*'''1511''' (''[[Eishō (Muromachi period)|Eishō]] 8, 2nd month''): When Kanetomo died at the age of 77, his passing was considered a significant event in the chronicles of the Imperial [[history of Japan]].<ref name="titsingh368">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834) {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 368.|page=368}}; aka Ourabe no Kane tomo</ref>
==Yoshida Shinto== The early period Shinto school founded by Kanetomo was called ''Genpon-Sōgen Shinto'' ("Shinto of the Original Founder"),<ref name="ito"/> also known as Yuiitsu Shintō ("Only one Shintō").<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto,'' p. 141.</ref> Prior to Kanetomo, the understanding and practice of Shinto was intermingled with [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]].<ref name="titsingh368"/> Sanetomo invested a lifetime in a process of disentangling what were thereafter construed as the two distinct entities.<ref name="ito"/>
===Inverted ''honji suijaku''=== {{Main|Honji suijaku}} The term ''[[honji suijaku]]'' expresses a Japanese [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] theory according to which a perceived Shinto ''[[kami]]'' is the manifestation of a Buddhist god.<ref>Satō Masato. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1356 "''Honji Suijaku Setsu'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007; ''Basic Terms of Shinto'', [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_h.html#honji-suijaku_setsu "Honji Suijaku Setsu,"] 1997.</ref> This theory proposed and presumed that the resulting dual entity would necessarily have a fundamental Buddhist core, and that any Shinto aspect was secondary.<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. [http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm "''Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami'';"] Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
In the late [[Kamakura period]], a counter-theory arose which also began with the notion of such dual entities; however, the counter-theorists construed that the ''kami'' side was primary and the Buddhist one was secondary. This came to be known as the ''Inverted honji suijaku ''.<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
Kanetomo was influenced by these ideas and brought them further. He proposed to set aside the conceptual theories of such entities.<ref>Shirayama, Yoshitarō. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1355 "''Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu'' ('Anti-Honjisuijaku thought')."] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007.</ref>
Up through the end of the [[Edo]] period, Kanetomo's followers and the [[Yoshida Shrine]] were granted the right to award Ranks to all shrines and priests except for those associated with the Imperial family.<ref>Breen, John et al. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 176.|page=176}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Shinto sects and schools]] * [[Kunitokotachi]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Bernhard Scheid|Scheid, Bernhard]]. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto.'' Vienna: ''Verlag der Österreichischen Akademie der Wissenschaften''. {{ISBN|9783700129899}}; {{OCLC|186460367}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''] (''[[Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''). Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}}
==External links== * [[Kokugakuin University]], ''Encyclopedia of Shinto,'' [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo"]

[http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm "''Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami'';"] Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>

Up through the end of the [[Edo]] period, Kanetomo's followers and the [[Yoshida Shrine]] were granted the right to award Ranks to all shrines and priests except for those associated with the Imperial family.<ref>Breen, John et al. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 176.|page=176}}</ref>

(2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto,'' p. 141.</ref> Prior to Kanetomo, the understanding and practice of Shinto was intermingled with [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]].<ref name="titsingh368"/> Sanetomo invested a lifetime in a process of disentangling what were thereafter construed as the two distinct entities.<ref name="ito"/>
===Inverted ''honji suijaku''=== {{Main|Honji suijaku}} The term ''[[honji suijaku]]'' expresses a Japanese [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] theory according to which a perceived Shinto ''[[kami]]'' is the manifestation of a Buddhist god.<ref>Satō Masato. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1356 "''Honji Suijaku Setsu'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007; ''Basic Terms of Shinto'', [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_h.html#honji-suijaku_setsu "Honji Suijaku Setsu,"] 1997.</ref> This theory proposed and presumed that the resulting dual entity would necessarily have a fundamental Buddhist core, and that any Shinto aspect was secondary.<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. [http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm "''Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami'';"] Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
In the late [[Kamakura period]], a counter-theory arose which also began with the notion of such dual entities; however, the counter-theorists construed that the ''kami'' side was primary and the Buddhist one was secondary. This came to be known as the ''Inverted honji suijaku ''.<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
Kanetomo was influenced by these ideas and brought them further. He proposed to set aside the conceptual theories of such entities.<ref>Shirayama, Yoshitarō. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1355 "''Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu'' ('Anti-Honjisuijaku thought')."] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007.</ref>
Up through the end of the [[Edo]] period, Kanetomo's followers and the [[Yoshida Shrine]] were granted the right to award Ranks to all shrines and priests except for those associated with the Imperial family.<ref>Breen, John et al. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 176.|page=176}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Shinto sects and schools]] * [[Kunitokotachi]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Bernhard Scheid|Scheid, Bernhard]]. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto.'' Vienna: ''Verlag der Österreichischen Akademie der Wissenschaften''. {{ISBN|9783700129899}}; {{OCLC|186460367}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''] (''[[Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''). Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}}

{{family name hatnote|Yoshida|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Nijuunisha-chuushiki.JPG|250 px|thumb|A copy of the Nijūnisha Chūshiki by Yoshida Kanetomo]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshida Kanetomo'''|吉田 兼倶||1435–1511}} was a Japanese [[Shinto]] priest of the [[Sengoku period]]. He was a seminal figure in the evolution of a coherent descriptive and interpretive [[Model (abstract)|schema]] of Shinto ritual and [[mythology]].<ref name="ito">Itō Satoshi. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo,"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' April 15, 2006.</ref>
==Career== Kanetomo progressed gradually through the Ranks of the Imperial offices in the [[Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs), which was one of the Imperial bureaucracies which were set up under the [[ritsuryō]] system in the 8th century. Kanetomo's eventually became an {{nihongo|Imperial Chamberlain|侍従|jijū}}. Other positions he held at different times were {{nihongo|Senior Assistant Director of Divinities|神祇大輔|jingi taifu}} and {{nihongo|Vice Director of the Board of Censors|弾正台弼|danjōdai hitsu}}.<ref name="ito"/>
*'''1511''' (''[[Eishō (Muromachi period)|Eishō]] 8, 2nd month''): When Kanetomo died at the age of 77, his passing was considered a significant event in the chronicles of the Imperial [[history of Japan]].<ref name="titsingh368">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834) {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 368.|page=368}}; aka Ourabe no Kane tomo</ref>
==Yoshida Shinto== The early period Shinto school founded by Kanetomo was called ''Genpon-Sōgen Shinto'' ("Shinto of the Original Founder"),<ref name="ito"/> also known as Yuiitsu Shintō ("Only one Shintō").<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto,'' p. 141.</ref> Prior to Kanetomo, the understanding and practice of Shinto was intermingled with [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]].<ref name="titsingh368"/> Sanetomo invested a lifetime in a process of disentangling what were thereafter construed as the two distinct entities.<ref name="ito"/>
===Inverted ''honji suijaku''=== {{Main|Honji suijaku}} The term ''[[honji suijaku]]'' expresses a Japanese [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] theory according to which a perceived Shinto ''[[kami]]'' is the manifestation of a Buddhist god.<ref>Satō Masato. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1356 "''Honji Suijaku Setsu'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007; ''Basic Terms of Shinto'', [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_h.html#honji-suijaku_setsu "Honji Suijaku Setsu,"] 1997.</ref> This theory proposed and presumed that the resulting dual entity would necessarily have a fundamental Buddhist core, and that any Shinto aspect was secondary.<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. [http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm "''Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami'';"] Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
In the late [[Kamakura period]], a counter-theory arose which also began with the notion of such dual entities; however, the counter-theorists construed that the ''kami'' side was primary and the Buddhist one was secondary. This came to be known as the ''Inverted honji suijaku ''.<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
Kanetomo was influenced by these ideas and brought them further. He proposed to set aside the conceptual theories of such entities.<ref>Shirayama, Yoshitarō. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1355 "''Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu'' ('Anti-Honjisuijaku thought')."] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007.</ref>
Up through the end of the [[Edo]] period, Kanetomo's followers and the [[Yoshida Shrine]] were granted the right to award Ranks to all shrines and priests except for those associated with the Imperial family.<ref>Breen, John et al. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 176.|page=176}}</ref>

[https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Bernhard Scheid|Scheid, Bernhard]]. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto.'' Vienna: ''Verlag der Österreichischen Akademie der Wissenschaften''. {{ISBN|9783700129899}}; {{OCLC|186460367}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''] (''[[Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''). In the late [[Kamakura period]], a counter-theory arose which also began with the notion of such dual entities; however, the counter-theorists construed that the ''kami'' side was primary and the Buddhist one was secondary. This came to be known as the ''Inverted honji suijaku ''.<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
Kanetomo was influenced by these ideas and brought them further. He proposed to set aside the conceptual theories of such entities.<ref>Shirayama, Yoshitarō. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1355 "''Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu'' ('Anti-Honjisuijaku thought')."] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007.</ref>
Up through the end of the [[:en:Edo|Edo]] period, Kanetomo's followers and the [[Yoshida Shrine]] were granted the right to award Ranks to all shrines and priests except for those associated with the Imperial family.<ref>Breen, John et al. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 176.|page=176}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Yoshida|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Nijuunisha-chuushiki.JPG|250 px|thumb|A copy of the Nijūnisha Chūshiki by Yoshida Kanetomo]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshida Kanetomo'''|吉田 兼倶||1435–1511}} was a Japanese [[Shinto]] priest of the [[Sengoku period]]. He was a seminal figure in the evolution of a coherent descriptive and interpretive [[Model (abstract)|schema]] of Shinto ritual and [[mythology]].<ref name="ito">Itō Satoshi. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo,"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' April 15, 2006.</ref>
==Career== Kanetomo progressed gradually through the Ranks of the Imperial offices in the [[Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs), which was one of the Imperial bureaucracies which were set up under the [[ritsuryō]] system in the 8th century. Kanetomo's eventually became an {{nihongo|Imperial Chamberlain|侍従|jijū}}. Other positions he held at different times were {{nihongo|Senior Assistant Director of Divinities|神祇大輔|jingi taifu}} and {{nihongo|Vice Director of the Board of Censors|弾正台弼|danjōdai hitsu}}.<ref name="ito"/>
*'''1511''' (''[[:en:Eishō (Muromachi period)|Eishō]] 8, 2nd month''): When Kanetomo died at the age of 77, his passing was considered a significant event in the chronicles of the Imperial [[history of Japan]].<ref name="titsingh368">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834) {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 368.|page=368}}; aka Ourabe no Kane tomo</ref>
==Yoshida Shinto== The early period Shinto school founded by Kanetomo was called ''Genpon-Sōgen Shinto'' ("Shinto of the Original Founder"),<ref name="ito"/> also known as Yuiitsu Shintō ("Only one Shintō").<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto,'' p. 141.</ref> Prior to Kanetomo, the understanding and practice of Shinto was intermingled with [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]].<ref name="titsingh368"/> Sanetomo invested a lifetime in a process of disentangling what were thereafter construed as the two distinct entities.<ref name="ito"/>
===Inverted ''honji suijaku''=== {{Main|Honji suijaku}} The term ''[[honji suijaku]]'' expresses a Japanese [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] theory according to which a perceived Shinto ''[[kami]]'' is the manifestation of a Buddhist god.<ref>Satō Masato. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1356 "''Honji Suijaku Setsu'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007; ''Basic Terms of Shinto'', [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_h.html#honji-suijaku_setsu "Honji Suijaku Setsu,"] 1997.</ref> This theory proposed and presumed that the resulting dual entity would necessarily have a fundamental Buddhist core, and that any Shinto aspect was secondary.<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. [http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm "''Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami'';"] Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
In the late [[Kamakura period]], a counter-theory arose which also began with the notion of such dual entities; however, the counter-theorists construed that the ''kami'' side was primary and the Buddhist one was secondary. This came to be known as the ''Inverted honji suijaku ''.<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
Kanetomo was influenced by these ideas and brought them further. He proposed to set aside the conceptual theories of such entities.<ref>Shirayama, Yoshitarō. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1355 "''Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu'' ('Anti-Honjisuijaku thought')."] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007.</ref>
Up through the end of the [[:en:Edo|Edo]] period, Kanetomo's followers and the [[Yoshida Shrine]] were granted the right to award Ranks to all shrines and priests except for those associated with the Imperial family.<ref>Breen, John et al. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 176.|page=176}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Shinto sects and schools]] * [[Kunitokotachi]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[:en:Bernhard Scheid|Scheid, Bernhard]]. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto.'' Vienna: ''Verlag der Österreichischen Akademie der Wissenschaften''. {{ISBN|9783700129899}}; {{OCLC|186460367}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''] (''[[Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''). Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}}

Up through the end of the [[:en:Edo|Edo]] period, Kanetomo's followers and the [[Yoshida Shrine]] were granted the right to award Ranks to all shrines and priests except for those associated with the Imperial family.<ref>Breen, John et al. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 176.|page=176}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Yoshida|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Nijuunisha-chuushiki.JPG|250 px|thumb|A copy of the Nijūnisha Chūshiki by Yoshida Kanetomo]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshida Kanetomo'''|吉田 兼倶||1435–1511}} was a Japanese [[Shinto]] priest of the [[Sengoku period]]. He was a seminal figure in the evolution of a coherent descriptive and interpretive [[Model (abstract)|schema]] of Shinto ritual and [[mythology]].<ref name="ito">Itō Satoshi. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo,"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' April 15, 2006.</ref>
==Career== Kanetomo progressed gradually through the Ranks of the Imperial offices in the [[Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs), which was one of the Imperial bureaucracies which were set up under the [[ritsuryō]] system in the 8th century. Kanetomo's eventually became an {{nihongo|Imperial Chamberlain|侍従|jijū}}. Other positions he held at different times were {{nihongo|Senior Assistant Director of Divinities|神祇大輔|jingi taifu}} and {{nihongo|Vice Director of the Board of Censors|弾正台弼|danjōdai hitsu}}.<ref name="ito"/>
*'''1511''' (''[[:en:Eishō (Muromachi period)|Eishō]] 8, 2nd month''): When Kanetomo died at the age of 77, his passing was considered a significant event in the chronicles of the Imperial [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]].<ref name="titsingh368">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834) {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 368.|page=368}}; aka Ourabe no Kane tomo</ref>
==Yoshida Shinto== The early period Shinto school founded by Kanetomo was called ''Genpon-Sōgen Shinto'' ("Shinto of the Original Founder"),<ref name="ito"/> also known as Yuiitsu Shintō ("Only one Shintō").<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto,'' p. 141.</ref> Prior to Kanetomo, the understanding and practice of Shinto was intermingled with [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]].<ref name="titsingh368"/> Sanetomo invested a lifetime in a process of disentangling what were thereafter construed as the two distinct entities.<ref name="ito"/>
===Inverted ''honji suijaku''=== {{Main|Honji suijaku}} The term ''[[honji suijaku]]'' expresses a Japanese [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] theory according to which a perceived Shinto ''[[kami]]'' is the manifestation of a Buddhist god.<ref>Satō Masato. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1356 "''Honji Suijaku Setsu'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007; ''Basic Terms of Shinto'', [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_h.html#honji-suijaku_setsu "Honji Suijaku Setsu,"] 1997.</ref> This theory proposed and presumed that the resulting dual entity would necessarily have a fundamental Buddhist core, and that any Shinto aspect was secondary.<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. [http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm "''Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami'';"] Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
In the late [[Kamakura period]], a counter-theory arose which also began with the notion of such dual entities; however, the counter-theorists construed that the ''kami'' side was primary and the Buddhist one was secondary. This came to be known as the ''Inverted honji suijaku ''.<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
Kanetomo was influenced by these ideas and brought them further. He proposed to set aside the conceptual theories of such entities.<ref>Shirayama, Yoshitarō. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1355 "''Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu'' ('Anti-Honjisuijaku thought')."] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007.</ref>
Up through the end of the [[:en:Edo|Edo]] period, Kanetomo's followers and the [[Yoshida Shrine]] were granted the right to award Ranks to all shrines and priests except for those associated with the Imperial family.<ref>Breen, John et al. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 176.|page=176}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Shinto sects and schools]] * [[Kunitokotachi]]

(2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto.'' Vienna: ''Verlag der Österreichischen Akademie der Wissenschaften''. {{ISBN|9783700129899}}; {{OCLC|186460367}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''] (''[[Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''). Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}}

(2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto.'' Vienna: ''Verlag der Österreichischen Akademie der Wissenschaften''. {{ISBN|9783700129899}}; {{OCLC|186460367}} * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''] (''[[Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''). revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Yoshida|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Nijuunisha-chuushiki.JPG|250 px|thumb|A copy of the Nijūnisha Chūshiki by Yoshida Kanetomo]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshida Kanetomo'''|吉田 兼倶||1435–1511}} was a Japanese [[Shinto]] priest of the [[Sengoku period]]. He was a seminal figure in the evolution of a coherent descriptive and interpretive [[Model (abstract)|schema]] of Shinto ritual and [[mythology]].<ref name="ito">Itō Satoshi. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo,"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' April 15, 2006.</ref>
==Career== Kanetomo progressed gradually through the Ranks of the Imperial offices in the [[Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs), which was one of the Imperial bureaucracies which were set up under the [[ritsuryō]] system in the 8th century. Kanetomo's eventually became an {{nihongo|Imperial Chamberlain|侍従|jijū}}. Other positions he held at different times were {{nihongo|Senior Assistant Director of Divinities|神祇大輔|jingi taifu}} and {{nihongo|Vice Director of the Board of Censors|弾正台弼|danjōdai hitsu}}.<ref name="ito"/>
*'''1511''' (''[[:en:Eishō (Muromachi period)|Eishō]] 8, 2nd month''): When Kanetomo died at the age of 77, his passing was considered a significant event in the chronicles of the Imperial [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]].<ref name="titsingh368">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834) {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 368.|page=368}}; aka Ourabe no Kane tomo</ref>
==Yoshida Shinto== The early period Shinto school founded by Kanetomo was called ''Genpon-Sōgen Shinto'' ("Shinto of the Original Founder"),<ref name="ito"/> also known as Yuiitsu Shintō ("Only one Shintō").<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto,'' p. 141.</ref> Prior to Kanetomo, the understanding and practice of Shinto was intermingled with [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]].<ref name="titsingh368"/> Sanetomo invested a lifetime in a process of disentangling what were thereafter construed as the two distinct entities.<ref name="ito"/>
===Inverted ''honji suijaku''=== {{Main|Honji suijaku}} The term ''[[honji suijaku]]'' expresses a Japanese [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] theory according to which a perceived Shinto ''[[kami]]'' is the manifestation of a Buddhist god.<ref>Satō Masato. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1356 "''Honji Suijaku Setsu'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007; ''Basic Terms of Shinto'', [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_h.html#honji-suijaku_setsu "Honji Suijaku Setsu,"] 1997.</ref> This theory proposed and presumed that the resulting dual entity would necessarily have a fundamental Buddhist core, and that any Shinto aspect was secondary.<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. [http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm "''Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami'';"] Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
In the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], a counter-theory arose which also began with the notion of such dual entities; however, the counter-theorists construed that the ''kami'' side was primary and the Buddhist one was secondary. This came to be known as the ''Inverted honji suijaku ''.<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
Kanetomo was influenced by these ideas and brought them further. He proposed to set aside the conceptual theories of such entities.<ref>Shirayama, Yoshitarō. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1355 "''Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu'' ('Anti-Honjisuijaku thought')."] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007.</ref>
Up through the end of the [[:en:Edo|Edo]] period, Kanetomo's followers and the [[Yoshida Shrine]] were granted the right to award Ranks to all shrines and priests except for those associated with the Imperial family.<ref>Breen, John et al. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 176.|page=176}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Shinto sects and schools]] * [[:en:Kunitokotachi|Kunitokotachi]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[:en:Bernhard Scheid|Scheid, Bernhard]]. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto.'' Vienna: ''Verlag der Österreichischen Akademie der Wissenschaften''. {{ISBN|9783700129899}}; {{OCLC|186460367}} * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''] (''[[Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''). Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}}
==External links== * [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]], ''Encyclopedia of Shinto,'' [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo"]

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[arz:يوشيدا كانيتومو]] [[de:Yoshida Kanetomo]] [[fr:Yoshida Kanetomo]] [[it:Yoshida Kanetomo]] [[ja:吉田兼倶]] [[pt:Yoshida Kanetomo]] [[ru:Ёсида, Канэтомо]] [[zh:吉田兼俱]]

revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Yoshida|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Nijuunisha-chuushiki.JPG|250 px|thumb|A copy of the Nijūnisha Chūshiki by Yoshida Kanetomo]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshida Kanetomo'''|吉田 兼倶||1435–1511}} was a Japanese [[Shinto]] priest of the [[Sengoku period]]. He was a seminal figure in the evolution of a coherent descriptive and interpretive [[:en:Model (abstract)|schema]] of Shinto ritual and [[:en:Myth|Myth]].<ref name="ito">Itō Satoshi. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo,"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' April 15, 2006.</ref>
==Career== Kanetomo progressed gradually through the Ranks of the Imperial offices in the [[Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs), which was one of the Imperial bureaucracies which were set up under the [[ritsuryō]] system in the 8th century. Kanetomo's eventually became an {{nihongo|Imperial Chamberlain|侍従|jijū}}. Other positions he held at different times were {{nihongo|Senior Assistant Director of Divinities|神祇大輔|jingi taifu}} and {{nihongo|Vice Director of the Board of Censors|弾正台弼|danjōdai hitsu}}.<ref name="ito"/>
*'''1511''' (''[[:en:Eishō (Muromachi period)|Eishō]] 8, 2nd month''): When Kanetomo died at the age of 77, his passing was considered a significant event in the chronicles of the Imperial [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]].<ref name="titsingh368">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834) {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 368.|page=368}}; aka Ourabe no Kane tomo</ref>
==Yoshida Shinto== The early period Shinto school founded by Kanetomo was called ''Genpon-Sōgen Shinto'' ("Shinto of the Original Founder"),<ref name="ito"/> also known as Yuiitsu Shintō ("Only one Shintō").<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto,'' p. 141.</ref> Prior to Kanetomo, the understanding and practice of Shinto was intermingled with [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]].<ref name="titsingh368"/> Sanetomo invested a lifetime in a process of disentangling what were thereafter construed as the two distinct entities.<ref name="ito"/>
===Inverted ''honji suijaku''=== {{Main|Honji suijaku}} The term ''[[honji suijaku]]'' expresses a Japanese [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] theory according to which a perceived Shinto ''[[kami]]'' is the manifestation of a Buddhist god.<ref>Satō Masato. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1356 "''Honji Suijaku Setsu'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007; ''Basic Terms of Shinto'', [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_h.html#honji-suijaku_setsu "Honji Suijaku Setsu,"] 1997.</ref> This theory proposed and presumed that the resulting dual entity would necessarily have a fundamental Buddhist core, and that any Shinto aspect was secondary.<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. [http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm "''Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami'';"] Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
In the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], a counter-theory arose which also began with the notion of such dual entities; however, the counter-theorists construed that the ''kami'' side was primary and the Buddhist one was secondary. This came to be known as the ''Inverted honji suijaku ''.<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
Kanetomo was influenced by these ideas and brought them further. He proposed to set aside the conceptual theories of such entities.<ref>Shirayama, Yoshitarō. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1355 "''Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu'' ('Anti-Honjisuijaku thought')."] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007.</ref>
Up through the end of the [[:en:Edo|Edo]] period, Kanetomo's followers and the [[Yoshida Shrine]] were granted the right to award Ranks to all shrines and priests except for those associated with the Imperial family.<ref>Breen, John et al. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 176.|page=176}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Shinto sects and schools]] * [[:en:Kunitokotachi|Kunitokotachi]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[:en:Bernhard Scheid|Scheid, Bernhard]]. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto.'' Vienna: ''Verlag der Österreichischen Akademie der Wissenschaften''. {{ISBN|9783700129899}}; {{OCLC|186460367}} * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''] (''[[:en:Nihon Odai Ichiran|Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''). Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}}
==External links== * [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]], ''Encyclopedia of Shinto,'' [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo"]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Yoshida|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Nijuunisha-chuushiki.JPG|250 px|thumb|A copy of the Nijūnisha Chūshiki by Yoshida Kanetomo]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshida Kanetomo'''|吉田 兼倶||1435–1511}} was a Japanese [[Shinto]] priest of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. He was a seminal figure in the evolution of a coherent descriptive and interpretive [[:en:Model (abstract)|schema]] of Shinto ritual and [[:en:Myth|Myth]].<ref name="ito">Itō Satoshi. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo,"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' April 15, 2006.</ref>
==Career== Kanetomo progressed gradually through the Ranks of the Imperial offices in the [[Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs), which was one of the Imperial bureaucracies which were set up under the [[ritsuryō]] system in the 8th century. Kanetomo's eventually became an {{nihongo|Imperial Chamberlain|侍従|jijū}}. Other positions he held at different times were {{nihongo|Senior Assistant Director of Divinities|神祇大輔|jingi taifu}} and {{nihongo|Vice Director of the Board of Censors|弾正台弼|danjōdai hitsu}}.<ref name="ito"/>
*'''1511''' (''[[:en:Eishō (Muromachi period)|Eishō]] 8, 2nd month''): When Kanetomo died at the age of 77, his passing was considered a significant event in the chronicles of the Imperial [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]].<ref name="titsingh368">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834) {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 368.|page=368}}; aka Ourabe no Kane tomo</ref>
==Yoshida Shinto== The early period Shinto school founded by Kanetomo was called ''Genpon-Sōgen Shinto'' ("Shinto of the Original Founder"),<ref name="ito"/> also known as Yuiitsu Shintō ("Only one Shintō").<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto,'' p. 141.</ref> Prior to Kanetomo, the understanding and practice of Shinto was intermingled with [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]].<ref name="titsingh368"/> Sanetomo invested a lifetime in a process of disentangling what were thereafter construed as the two distinct entities.<ref name="ito"/>
===Inverted ''honji suijaku''=== {{Main|Honji suijaku}} The term ''[[honji suijaku]]'' expresses a Japanese [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] theory according to which a perceived Shinto ''[[kami]]'' is the manifestation of a Buddhist god.<ref>Satō Masato. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1356 "''Honji Suijaku Setsu'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007; ''Basic Terms of Shinto'', [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_h.html#honji-suijaku_setsu "Honji Suijaku Setsu,"] 1997.</ref> This theory proposed and presumed that the resulting dual entity would necessarily have a fundamental Buddhist core, and that any Shinto aspect was secondary.<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. [http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm "''Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami'';"] Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
In the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], a counter-theory arose which also began with the notion of such dual entities; however, the counter-theorists construed that the ''kami'' side was primary and the Buddhist one was secondary. This came to be known as the ''Inverted honji suijaku ''.<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
Kanetomo was influenced by these ideas and brought them further. He proposed to set aside the conceptual theories of such entities.<ref>Shirayama, Yoshitarō. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1355 "''Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu'' ('Anti-Honjisuijaku thought')."] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007.</ref>
Up through the end of the [[:en:Edo|Edo]] period, Kanetomo's followers and the [[:en:Yoshida Shrine|Yoshida Shrine]] were granted the right to award Ranks to all shrines and priests except for those associated with the Imperial family.<ref>Breen, John et al. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 176.|page=176}}</ref>
==See also== * [[:en:Shinto sects and schools|Shinto sects and schools]] * [[:en:Kunitokotachi|Kunitokotachi]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[:en:Bernhard Scheid|Scheid, Bernhard]]. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto.'' Vienna: ''Verlag der Österreichischen Akademie der Wissenschaften''. {{ISBN|9783700129899}}; {{OCLC|186460367}} * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''] (''[[:en:Nihon Odai Ichiran|Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''). Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}}
==External links== * [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]], ''Encyclopedia of Shinto,'' [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo"]

Up through the end of the [[:en:Edo|Edo]] period, Kanetomo's followers and the [[:en:Yoshida Shrine|Yoshida Shrine]] were granted the right to award Ranks to all shrines and priests except for those associated with the Imperial family.<ref>Breen, John et al. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 176.|page=176}}</ref>

(2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto.'' Vienna: ''Verlag der Österreichischen Akademie der Wissenschaften''. {{ISBN|9783700129899}}; {{OCLC|186460367}} * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). revision.text:
{{Infobox person|image=File:吉田兼倶肖像画.jpg|birth_date=1435|death_date=1511; {{death date|1511|3|18}}|birth_place={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|death_place={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|kannushi|qid=Q60523}}|title={{ill|Q17210025|qid=Q17210025}}; {{ill|Q17212391|qid=Q17212391}}|children={{ill|Kiyohara no Nobukata|qid=Q6536137}}; {{ill|Q109321358|qid=Q109321358}}; {{ill|Q109795531|qid=Q109795531}}; {{ill|Q109795616|qid=Q109795616}}|father={{ill|Q109321337|qid=Q109321337}}|family={{ill|Yoshida family|qid=Q11413722}}|notable_works={{ill|Q11418709|qid=Q11418709}}; {{ill|Q11590922|qid=Q11590922}}|religion={{ill|Yoshida Shintō|qid=Q1760351}}|native_name=吉田兼倶|given_name={{ill|Kanetomo|qid=Q125469590}}|resting_place={{ill|Yoshida Shrine|qid=Q713177}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q723266]] Wikidata name: Yoshida Kanetomo [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{family name hatnote|Yoshida|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Nijuunisha-chuushiki.JPG|250 px|thumb|A copy of the Nijūnisha Chūshiki by Yoshida Kanetomo]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshida Kanetomo'''|吉田 兼倶||1435–1511}} was a Japanese [[Shinto]] priest of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. He was a seminal figure in the evolution of a coherent descriptive and interpretive [[:en:Model (abstract)|schema]] of Shinto ritual and [[:en:Myth|Myth]].<ref name="ito">Itō Satoshi. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo,"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' April 15, 2006.</ref>
==Career== Kanetomo progressed gradually through the Ranks of the Imperial offices in the [[Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs), which was one of the Imperial bureaucracies which were set up under the [[ritsuryō]] system in the 8th century. Kanetomo's eventually became an {{nihongo|Imperial Chamberlain|侍従|jijū}}. Other positions he held at different times were {{nihongo|Senior Assistant Director of Divinities|神祇大輔|jingi taifu}} and {{nihongo|Vice Director of the Board of Censors|弾正台弼|danjōdai hitsu}}.<ref name="ito"/>
*'''1511''' (''[[:en:Eishō (Muromachi period)|Eishō]] 8, 2nd month''): When Kanetomo died at the age of 77, his passing was considered a significant event in the chronicles of the Imperial [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]].<ref name="titsingh368">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834) {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 368.|page=368}}; aka Ourabe no Kane tomo</ref>
==Yoshida Shinto== The early period Shinto school founded by Kanetomo was called ''Genpon-Sōgen Shinto'' ("Shinto of the Original Founder"),<ref name="ito"/> also known as Yuiitsu Shintō ("Only one Shintō").<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto,'' p. 141.</ref> Prior to Kanetomo, the understanding and practice of Shinto was intermingled with [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]].<ref name="titsingh368"/> Sanetomo invested a lifetime in a process of disentangling what were thereafter construed as the two distinct entities.<ref name="ito"/>
===Inverted ''honji suijaku''=== {{Main|Honji suijaku}} The term ''[[honji suijaku]]'' expresses a Japanese [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] theory according to which a perceived Shinto ''[[kami]]'' is the manifestation of a Buddhist god.<ref>Satō Masato. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1356 "''Honji Suijaku Setsu'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007; ''Basic Terms of Shinto'', [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_h.html#honji-suijaku_setsu "Honji Suijaku Setsu,"] 1997.</ref> This theory proposed and presumed that the resulting dual entity would necessarily have a fundamental Buddhist core, and that any Shinto aspect was secondary.<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. [http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm "''Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami'';"] Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
In the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], a counter-theory arose which also began with the notion of such dual entities; however, the counter-theorists construed that the ''kami'' side was primary and the Buddhist one was secondary. This came to be known as the ''Inverted honji suijaku ''.<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
Kanetomo was influenced by these ideas and brought them further. He proposed to set aside the conceptual theories of such entities.<ref>Shirayama, Yoshitarō. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1355 "''Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu'' ('Anti-Honjisuijaku thought')."] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007.</ref>
Up through the end of the [[:en:Edo|Edo]] period, Kanetomo's followers and the [[:en:Yoshida Shrine|Yoshida Shrine]] were granted the right to award Ranks to all shrines and priests except for those associated with the Imperial family.<ref>Breen, John et al. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 176.|page=176}}</ref>
==See also== * [[:en:Shinto sects and schools|Shinto sects and schools]] * [[:en:Kunitokotachi|Kunitokotachi]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[:en:Bernhard Scheid|Scheid, Bernhard]]. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto.'' Vienna: ''Verlag der Österreichischen Akademie der Wissenschaften''. {{ISBN|9783700129899}}; {{OCLC|186460367}} * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''] (''[[:en:Nihon Odai Ichiran|Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''). Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}}
==External links== * [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]], ''Encyclopedia of Shinto,'' [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo"]

revision.text:
{{Infobox person|image=File:吉田兼倶肖像画.jpg|birth_date=1435|death_date=1511; {{death date|1511|3|18}}|birth_place={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|death_place={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|kannushi|qid=Q60523}}|title={{ill|Q17210025|qid=Q17210025}}; {{ill|Q17212391|qid=Q17212391}}|children={{ill|Kiyohara no Nobukata|qid=Q6536137}}; {{ill|Q109321358|qid=Q109321358}}; {{ill|Q109795531|qid=Q109795531}}; {{ill|Q109795616|qid=Q109795616}}|father={{ill|Q109321337|qid=Q109321337}}|family={{ill|Yoshida family|qid=Q11413722}}|notable_works={{ill|Q11418709|qid=Q11418709}}; {{ill|Q11590922|qid=Q11590922}}|religion={{ill|Yoshida Shintō|qid=Q1760351}}|native_name=吉田兼倶|given_name={{ill|Kanetomo|qid=Q125469590}}|resting_place={{ill|Yoshida Shrine|qid=Q713177}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q723266]] Wikidata name: Yoshida Kanetomo [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{family name hatnote|Yoshida|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Nijuunisha-chuushiki.JPG|250 px|thumb|A copy of the Nijūnisha Chūshiki by Yoshida Kanetomo]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshida Kanetomo'''|吉田 兼倶||1435–1511}} was a Japanese [[Shinto]] priest of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. He was a seminal figure in the evolution of a coherent descriptive and interpretive [[:en:Model (abstract)|schema]] of Shinto ritual and [[:en:Myth|Myth]].<ref name="ito">Itō Satoshi. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo,"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' April 15, 2006.</ref>
==Career== Kanetomo progressed gradually through the Ranks of the Imperial offices in the [[Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs), which was one of the Imperial bureaucracies which were set up under the [[ritsuryō]] system in the 8th century. Kanetomo's eventually became an {{nihongo|Imperial Chamberlain|侍従|jijū}}. Other positions he held at different times were {{nihongo|Senior Assistant Director of Divinities|神祇大輔|jingi taifu}} and {{nihongo|Vice Director of the Board of Censors|弾正台弼|danjōdai hitsu}}.<ref name="ito"/>
*'''1511''' (''[[:en:Eishō (Muromachi period)|Eishō]] 8, 2nd month''): When Kanetomo died at the age of 77, his passing was considered a significant event in the chronicles of the Imperial [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]].<ref name="titsingh368">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834) {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 368.|page=368}}; aka Ourabe no Kane tomo</ref>
==Yoshida Shinto== The early period Shinto school founded by Kanetomo was called ''Genpon-Sōgen Shinto'' ("Shinto of the Original Founder"),<ref name="ito"/> also known as Yuiitsu Shintō ("Only one Shintō").<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto,'' p. 141.</ref> Prior to Kanetomo, the understanding and practice of Shinto was intermingled with [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]].<ref name="titsingh368"/> Sanetomo invested a lifetime in a process of disentangling what were thereafter construed as the two distinct entities.<ref name="ito"/>
===Inverted ''honji suijaku''=== {{Main|Honji suijaku}} The term ''[[honji suijaku]]'' expresses a Japanese [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] theory according to which a perceived Shinto ''[[kami]]'' is the manifestation of a Buddhist god.<ref>Satō Masato. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1356 "''Honji Suijaku Setsu'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007; ''Basic Terms of Shinto'', [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_h.html#honji-suijaku_setsu "Honji Suijaku Setsu,"] 1997.</ref> This theory proposed and presumed that the resulting dual entity would necessarily have a fundamental Buddhist core, and that any Shinto aspect was secondary.<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. [http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm "''Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami'';"] Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
In the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], a counter-theory arose which also began with the notion of such dual entities; however, the counter-theorists construed that the ''kami'' side was primary and the Buddhist one was secondary. This came to be known as the ''Inverted honji suijaku ''.<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
Kanetomo was influenced by these ideas and brought them further. He proposed to set aside the conceptual theories of such entities.<ref>Shirayama, Yoshitarō. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1355 "''Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu'' ('Anti-Honjisuijaku thought')."] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007.</ref>
Up through the end of the [[:en:Edo|Edo]] period, Kanetomo's followers and the [[:en:Yoshida Shrine|Yoshida Shrine]] were granted the right to award Ranks to all shrines and priests except for those associated with the Imperial family.<ref>Breen, John et al. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 176.|page=176}}</ref>
==See also== * [[:en:Shinto sects and schools|Shinto sects and schools]] * [[:en:Kunitokotachi|Kunitokotachi]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[:en:Bernhard Scheid|Scheid, Bernhard]]. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto.'' Vienna: ''Verlag der Österreichischen Akademie der Wissenschaften''. {{ISBN|9783700129899}}; {{OCLC|186460367}} * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''] (''[[:en:Nihon Odai Ichiran|Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''). Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}}
==External links== * [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]], ''Encyclopedia of Shinto,'' [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo"]
[[en:Yoshida Kanetomo]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:يوشيدا كانيتومو]][[de:Yoshida Kanetomo]][[en:Yoshida Kanetomo]][[fr:Yoshida Kanetomo]][[ja:吉田兼倶]][[pt:Yoshida Kanetomo]][[ru:Ёсида Канэтомо]][[zh:吉田兼俱]] {{Infobox person|image=File:吉田兼倶肖像画.jpg|birth_date=1435|death_date=1511; {{death date|1511|3|18}}|birth_place={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|death_place={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|kannushi|WD=Q60523}}|title={{ill|Q17210025|WD=Q17210025}}; {{ill|Q17212391|WD=Q17212391}}|children={{ill|Kiyohara no Nobukata|WD=Q6536137}}; {{ill|Q109321358|WD=Q109321358}}; {{ill|Q109795531|WD=Q109795531}}; {{ill|Q109795616|WD=Q109795616}}|father={{ill|Q109321337|WD=Q109321337}}|family={{ill|Yoshida family|WD=Q11413722}}|notable_works={{ill|Q11418709|WD=Q11418709}}; {{ill|Q11590922|WD=Q11590922}}|religion={{ill|Yoshida Shintō|WD=Q1760351}}|native_name=吉田兼倶|given_name={{ill|Kanetomo|WD=Q125469590}}|resting_place={{ill|Yoshida Shrine|WD=Q713177}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q723266]] Wikidata name: Yoshida Kanetomo [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{family name hatnote|Yoshida|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Nijuunisha-chuushiki.JPG|250 px|thumb|A copy of the Nijūnisha Chūshiki by Yoshida Kanetomo]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshida Kanetomo'''|吉田 兼倶||1435–1511}} was a Japanese [[Shinto]] priest of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. He was a seminal figure in the evolution of a coherent descriptive and interpretive [[:en:Model (abstract)|schema]] of Shinto ritual and [[:en:Myth|Myth]].<ref name="ito">Itō Satoshi. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo,"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' April 15, 2006.</ref>
==Career== Kanetomo progressed gradually through the Ranks of the Imperial offices in the [[Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs), which was one of the Imperial bureaucracies which were set up under the [[ritsuryō]] system in the 8th century. Kanetomo's eventually became an {{nihongo|Imperial Chamberlain|侍従|jijū}}. Other positions he held at different times were {{nihongo|Senior Assistant Director of Divinities|神祇大輔|jingi taifu}} and {{nihongo|Vice Director of the Board of Censors|弾正台弼|danjōdai hitsu}}.<ref name="ito"/>
*'''1511''' (''[[:en:Eishō (Muromachi period)|Eishō]] 8, 2nd month''): When Kanetomo died at the age of 77, his passing was considered a significant event in the chronicles of the Imperial [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]].<ref name="titsingh368">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834) {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 368.|page=368}}; aka Ourabe no Kane tomo</ref>
==Yoshida Shinto== The early period Shinto school founded by Kanetomo was called ''Genpon-Sōgen Shinto'' ("Shinto of the Original Founder"),<ref name="ito"/> also known as Yuiitsu Shintō ("Only one Shintō").<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto,'' p. 141.</ref> Prior to Kanetomo, the understanding and practice of Shinto was intermingled with [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]].<ref name="titsingh368"/> Sanetomo invested a lifetime in a process of disentangling what were thereafter construed as the two distinct entities.<ref name="ito"/>
===Inverted ''honji suijaku''=== {{Main|Honji suijaku}} The term ''[[honji suijaku]]'' expresses a Japanese [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] theory according to which a perceived Shinto ''[[kami]]'' is the manifestation of a Buddhist god.<ref>Satō Masato. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1356 "''Honji Suijaku Setsu'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007; ''Basic Terms of Shinto'', [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_h.html#honji-suijaku_setsu "Honji Suijaku Setsu,"] 1997.</ref> This theory proposed and presumed that the resulting dual entity would necessarily have a fundamental Buddhist core, and that any Shinto aspect was secondary.<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. [http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm "''Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami'';"] Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
In the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], a counter-theory arose which also began with the notion of such dual entities; however, the counter-theorists construed that the ''kami'' side was primary and the Buddhist one was secondary. This came to be known as the ''Inverted honji suijaku ''.<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
Kanetomo was influenced by these ideas and brought them further. He proposed to set aside the conceptual theories of such entities.<ref>Shirayama, Yoshitarō. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1355 "''Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu'' ('Anti-Honjisuijaku thought')."] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007.</ref>
Up through the end of the [[:en:Edo|Edo]] period, Kanetomo's followers and the [[:en:Yoshida Shrine|Yoshida Shrine]] were granted the right to award Ranks to all shrines and priests except for those associated with the Imperial family.<ref>Breen, John et al. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 176.|page=176}}</ref>
==See also== * [[:en:Shinto sects and schools|Shinto sects and schools]] * [[:en:Kunitokotachi|Kunitokotachi]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[:en:Bernhard Scheid|Scheid, Bernhard]]. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto.'' Vienna: ''Verlag der Österreichischen Akademie der Wissenschaften''. {{ISBN|9783700129899}}; {{OCLC|186460367}} * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''] (''[[:en:Nihon Odai Ichiran|Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''). Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}}
==External links== * [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]], ''Encyclopedia of Shinto,'' [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo"]
[[en:Yoshida Kanetomo]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[arz:يوشيدا كانيتومو]] [[de:Yoshida Kanetomo]] [[fr:Yoshida Kanetomo]] [[it:Yoshida Kanetomo]] [[ja:吉田兼倶]] [[pt:Yoshida Kanetomo]] [[ru:Ёсида, Канэтомо]] [[zh:吉田兼俱]] {{wikidata link|Q723266}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:يوشيدا كانيتومو]][[de:Yoshida Kanetomo]][[en:Yoshida Kanetomo]][[fr:Yoshida Kanetomo]][[ja:吉田兼倶]][[pt:Yoshida Kanetomo]][[ru:Ёсида Канэтомо]][[zh:吉田兼俱]] {{Infobox person|image=File:吉田兼倶肖像画.jpg|birth_date=1435|death_date=1511; {{death date|1511|3|18}}|birth_place={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|death_place={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|kannushi|WD=Q60523}}|title={{ill|Q17210025|WD=Q17210025}}; {{ill|Q17212391|WD=Q17212391}}|children={{ill|Kiyohara no Nobukata|WD=Q6536137}}; {{ill|Q109321358|WD=Q109321358}}; {{ill|Q109795531|WD=Q109795531}}; {{ill|Q109795616|WD=Q109795616}}|father={{ill|Q109321337|WD=Q109321337}}|family={{ill|Yoshida family|WD=Q11413722}}|notable_works={{ill|Q11418709|WD=Q11418709}}; {{ill|Q11590922|WD=Q11590922}}|religion={{ill|Yoshida Shintō|WD=Q1760351}}|native_name=吉田兼倶|given_name={{ill|Kanetomo|WD=Q125469590}}|resting_place={{ill|Yoshida Shrine|WD=Q713177}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q723266]] Wikidata name: Yoshida Kanetomo [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{family name hatnote|Yoshida|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Nijuunisha-chuushiki.JPG|250 px|thumb|A copy of the Nijūnisha Chūshiki by Yoshida Kanetomo]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshida Kanetomo'''|吉田 兼倶||1435–1511}} was a Japanese [[Shinto]] priest of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. He was a seminal figure in the evolution of a coherent descriptive and interpretive [[:en:Model (abstract)|schema]] of Shinto ritual and [[:en:Myth|Myth]].<ref name="ito">Itō Satoshi. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo,"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' April 15, 2006.</ref>
==Career== Kanetomo progressed gradually through the Ranks of the Imperial offices in the [[Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs), which was one of the Imperial bureaucracies which were set up under the [[ritsuryō]] system in the 8th century. Kanetomo's eventually became an {{nihongo|Imperial Chamberlain|侍従|jijū}}. Other positions he held at different times were {{nihongo|Senior Assistant Director of Divinities|神祇大輔|jingi taifu}} and {{nihongo|Vice Director of the Board of Censors|弾正台弼|danjōdai hitsu}}.<ref name="ito"/>
*'''1511''' (''[[:en:Eishō (Muromachi period)|Eishō]] 8, 2nd month''): When Kanetomo died at the age of 77, his passing was considered a significant event in the chronicles of the Imperial [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]].<ref name="titsingh368">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834) {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 368.|page=368}}; aka Ourabe no Kane tomo</ref>
==Yoshida Shinto== The early period Shinto school founded by Kanetomo was called ''Genpon-Sōgen Shinto'' ("Shinto of the Original Founder"),<ref name="ito"/> also known as Yuiitsu Shintō ("Only one Shintō").<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto,'' p. 141.</ref> Prior to Kanetomo, the understanding and practice of Shinto was intermingled with [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]].<ref name="titsingh368"/> Sanetomo invested a lifetime in a process of disentangling what were thereafter construed as the two distinct entities.<ref name="ito"/>
===Inverted ''honji suijaku''=== {{Main|Honji suijaku}} The term ''[[honji suijaku]]'' expresses a Japanese [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] theory according to which a perceived Shinto ''[[kami]]'' is the manifestation of a Buddhist god.<ref>Satō Masato. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1356 "''Honji Suijaku Setsu'',"] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007; ''Basic Terms of Shinto'', [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_h.html#honji-suijaku_setsu "Honji Suijaku Setsu,"] 1997.</ref> This theory proposed and presumed that the resulting dual entity would necessarily have a fundamental Buddhist core, and that any Shinto aspect was secondary.<ref>Scheid, Bernhard. [http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/gesch/honjisuijaku.htm "''Honji suijaku: Die Angleichung von Buddhas und Kami'';"] Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
In the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], a counter-theory arose which also began with the notion of such dual entities; however, the counter-theorists construed that the ''kami'' side was primary and the Buddhist one was secondary. This came to be known as the ''Inverted honji suijaku ''.<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teuween. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 95.|page=95}}</ref>
Kanetomo was influenced by these ideas and brought them further. He proposed to set aside the conceptual theories of such entities.<ref>Shirayama, Yoshitarō. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1355 "''Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu'' ('Anti-Honjisuijaku thought')."] ''Encyclopedia of Shinto.'' March 30, 2007.</ref>
Up through the end of the [[:en:Edo|Edo]] period, Kanetomo's followers and the [[:en:Yoshida Shrine|Yoshida Shrine]] were granted the right to award Ranks to all shrines and priests except for those associated with the Imperial family.<ref>Breen, John et al. (2000). {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 176.|page=176}}</ref>
==See also== * [[:en:Shinto sects and schools|Shinto sects and schools]] * [[:en:Kunitokotachi|Kunitokotachi]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|30em}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[:en:Bernhard Scheid|Scheid, Bernhard]]. (2001). ''Der eine und einzige Weg der Götter: Yoshida Kanetomo und die Erfindung des Shinto.'' Vienna: ''Verlag der Österreichischen Akademie der Wissenschaften''. {{ISBN|9783700129899}}; {{OCLC|186460367}} * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''] (''[[:en:Nihon Odai Ichiran|Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''). Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}}
==External links== * [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]], ''Encyclopedia of Shinto,'' [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=585 "Yoshida Kanetomo"]
